prose_hint: Have them speak of the slow fading of the Elves and the lands they must leave, with a sorrow worn smooth by long years.
Have them bring up an age-old loss — a drowned realm, a kinsman long dead — as though it were last night.
Have them turn the talk to the call of the Sea and the road West: who will take ship, and who will stay and dwindle.
Have them probe courteously, measuring the traveller's purpose and heart before they give any trust.
Have them ask quietly about the growing shadow, and rumour of orcs, wolves and fouler things abroad.
Have him speak of a mansion or mine lost to dragon or goblin, and the wealth and kin buried with it.
Have him talk of fine work, rich seams and fair trade — gold, gems and the worth of things — and speak of them bluntly.
Have him bring up a grievance nursed for generations — a slight, a debt, an enemy not yet repaid.
Have him speak of loyalty to king and folk, and the binding weight of a word once given.
Have him put short, suspicious questions, saying little of his own roads and liking prying even less.
Have them talk of the next meal, good ale, a warm fire and a soft bed — the real business of life.
Have them chatter about their hole, their garden, their kin and the doings of the next farm over.
Have them share who said what to whom, and the small scandals and rumours of the country round.
Have them show polite unease at strangers and the wide world, wishing adventures would happen elsewhere.
Have them offer food, a tale or directions, with homely, unsuspecting goodwill.
Have them trade news of the road — war, bandits, strange sights — hungry for word from elsewhere.
Have them talk plainly as working folk do: crops, beasts, the turning season, hard times or good.
Have them ask frankly but carefully who the traveller is, and where they are going, and why.
Have them raise a trouble of their own — a beast, a feud, a missing kinsman — and hope the stranger might aid it.
Have them probe in a guarded, even hostile way, mistrusting an unknown face in hard times.
Have them speak words that carry more than they say — a warning, a riddle, or counsel from one far older than he seems.
Have them turn the talk to the great shape of things: the slow war against the Shadow, and each one's small part in it.
Have them turn the talk to beasts, trees and growing things, and the keeping of them.
Have them put a strange, searching question that seems to weigh the traveller's worth or errand.
Have them offer aid on terms of their own — a gift, a path, a rescue — odd and not freely explained.
Give them no real talk — only snarled threats, jeering, and the promise of a cruel end.
Have them bicker and turn on one another over loot, orders or who eats first, overheard by the traveller.
Have them fling harsh demands at a captive — who are you, who sent you, what do you carry — backed by the lash.
